coredata.dc:title: A selective and molecular imaging approach for anticancer drug: Pemetrexed by nanoparticle accelerated molecularly imprinting polymer
coredata.dc:creator: Ozcelikay, Goksu
Karadas-Bakirhan, Nurgul
Taskin-Tok, Tugba
Ozkan, Sibel A.
coredata.dc:description: A new molecularly imprinting polymer (MIP) based sensor was developed for the selective detection of pemetrexed (PMX) by the electropolymerization of ortho phenylenediammine (o-PD) on carbon quantum dot modified screen printed carbon electrode (CQD/SPCE). The surface characterization was performed with cyclic voltammetry (CV), electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM). The conditions such as casting amount of CQD, incubation time, removal time for successful polymerization were tested. The selectivity of developed sensor also tested with similar compounds with PMX. The results showed that the developed sensor could achieve to detection of PMX from synthetic serum sample with high recovery 99.3%. From the computational perspective of view, PMX was also optimized at B3LYP/6-311 G∗//DFT level using the Gaussian 09 program package. Molecular electrostatic potential surface (MESP) was performed to investigate effects of the electronic and structure properties of PMX.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Molecularly imprinted polymer
Molecular imaging
Anticancer drug
Carbon quantum dots
Voltammetry

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Optimization of conditions for developing sensitive and selective MIP based sensor
Characterization of developed MIP based sensor
Precision and shelf-life of the MIP based sensor

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: CVs of (A)PMX-MIPs/SPCE in the potential range from 0 V to + 0.8 V in 0.1 molL−1 acetate buffer (at pH 5.2) containing 0.5 mmolL−1 PMX for 20 cycles (B)NIPs/SPCE in the potential range from 0 V to + 0.8 V in 0.1 molL−1 acetate buffer (at pH 5.2) for 20 cycles.
The possible binding mechanism of poly o-PD and PMX.
CVs of the preparation process of the o-PD terminated monolayer in 0.1 molL−1 acetate buffer (at pH 5.2) containing 0.5 mmolL−1 PMX for different cycles.

a) Effect of monomer: template ratio on the response of the MIP. b) Effect of removal time on the response of the MIP. The response was measured through DPV of 5.0 mmolL−1 Fe(CN)6 3-/4- solution after removal of the PMX. Scan rate 50 mV s−1.

Effect of rebinding time on the response of the MIP. The response was measured through DPV of 5.0 mmolL−1 Fe(CN)6 3-/4- solution after immersion of the electrode in a 0.1 mmolL−1 PMX solution for 10 min. Scan rate 50 mV s−1.

CV measurements (a) and Nyquist plots (b) for Bare SPCE; CQDs/SPCE; MIP/CQD/SPCE after EP; MIP/CQD/SPCE after PMX removal; MIP/CQD/SPCE after PMX rebinding in 5.0 mmolL−1 Fe(CN)6 3-/4- at a ratio of 1:1 and 0.1 molL−1 KCl at a scan rate of 50 mV s-1.
SEM images of bare/SPCE (a) CQDs/SPCE (b), MIP/CQDs/SPCE (c) and (d)TEM images of CQDs/SPCE.

Comparison of MIP/CQD/SPCE and NIP/CQD/SPCE (A) Relationship between peak current and PMX concentration (5,10,20,50,100 nmolL−1 PMX) on the NIP/CQD/SPCE (B) Relationship between peak current and PMX concentration (5,10,20,50,100 nmolL−1 PMX) on the MIP/CQD/SPCE (C).
(A) DPVs of different concentration of PMX (1, 2, 5, 10, 20 nmolL−1 PMX); (B) relationship between peak current and PMX concentration on the MIP/CQD/SPCE in synthetic serum sample.
The optimized structure (left side) and electron density isosurface (right side) for PMX calculated by B3LYP method with 6-311G(d) basis set.
Regression data of the calibration line for quantitative determination of PMX with developed MIP based sensor.
The recovery study for PMX in synthetic serum analysis.
The comparison of some validation parameters according to literature studies.

head.ce:title: A selective and molecular imaging approach for anticancer drug: Pemetrexed by nanoparticle accelerated molecularly imprinting polymer

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Ankara University, Faculty of Pharmacy, Department of Analytical Chemistry, Tandogan, Ankara, Turkey
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Ankara University, Faculty of Pharmacy, Department of Analytical Chemistry, Tandogan, Ankara, Turkey

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: University of Health Science, Gulhane Faculty of Pharmacy, Department of Analytical Chemistry, Ankara, Turkey
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: University of Health Science, Gulhane Faculty of Pharmacy, Department of Analytical Chemistry, Ankara, Turkey

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Arts and Sciences, Department of Chemistry, Gaziantep University, 27310, Gaziantep, Turkey

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Health Sciences, Department of Bioinformatics and Computational Biology, Gaziantep University, 27310, Gaziantep, Turkey

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Bioinformatics and Computational Biology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Health Sciences, Department of Bioinformatics and Computational Biology, Gaziantep University, 27310, Gaziantep, Turkey

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A new molecularly imprinting polymer (MIP) based sensor was developed for the selective detection of pemetrexed (PMX) by the electropolymerization of ortho phenylenediammine (o-PD) on carbon quantum dot modified screen printed carbon electrode (CQD/SPCE). The surface characterization was performed with cyclic voltammetry (CV), electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM). The conditions such as casting amount of CQD, incubation time, removal time for successful polymerization were tested. The selectivity of developed sensor also tested with similar compounds with PMX. The results showed that the developed sensor could achieve to detection of PMX from synthetic serum sample with high recovery 99.3%. From the computational perspective of view, PMX was also optimized at B3LYP/6-311 G∗//DFT level using the Gaussian 09 program package. Molecular electrostatic potential surface (MESP) was performed to investigate effects of the electronic and structure properties of PMX.

ce:section.ce:para: Tumors are characterized by a set of histological changes displayed by the tumor cells themselves. The general term used to describe a tumor is neoplasm, the literal meaning being “new growth”. A neoplasm refers to an abnormal mass of cells with uncoordinated or deregulated growth or cellular proliferation. Cancer, also called malignancy, is an abnormal growth of cells. Cancer cell progression depends upon the ability of cells to invade into surrounding tissue. Nowadays, more than 100 cancer types are described in the literature [1–3]. Symptoms can be seen depending on the cancer type. Cancer treatment may include chemotherapy, radiation, and/or surgery or their combination.

PMX is treat certain types of cancer medicine that used for the treatment of lung cancer or mesothelioma. PMX can administer to the human body by injection into a vein. It sometimes use with the combination with other cancer drugs. PMX may cause serious side effects and allergic reaction [1–4].

Various analytical approaches have been reported for the determination of PMX using UV [5], HPLC-UV [6–9], and HPLC-MS or MS/MS detection [9–16], in biological fluids and/or drug dosage form. However, such techniques generally require high cost instrumentation, complex sample preparations, and skilled labor to operate the tools and mostly these techniques are also lack of required sensitivity for the low amount detection of PMX in biological samples.

Electroanalytical methods allow precise identification of drugs and related molecules in biological fluids and pharmaceutical samples. Advances in electrochemical techniques for drug analysis are due to their simplicity, inexpensive assay and rapid response compared to other methods [17–21]. Electroanalytical chemistry attracts growing interest in the sensor development techniques as an alternating or supplementary method to spectrophotometric and separation methods due to its high sensitivity, fast response and reasonable application cost comparing to the other techniques. Especially using modified electrodes as sensors are much better in terms of the reversibility and kinetics of the electrode processes comparing to the unmodified working electrode. Nowadays, electroanalytical sensors commonly employ to produce easy-to-use, compact, and inexpensive devices for the sensitive assay of pharmaceutically active compounds from biological samples and/or in their dosage forms. Electrochemical sensors, an important subclass of chemical sensors, which are used as the transduction elements on the electrode. These types of devices have reached the commercial stage, and have found a vast range of important applications in the fields of pharmaceuticals, clinical, and environmental analyses.

During the past decades, for increasing selectivity and the specificity of the electroanalytical methods, MIP begin to use. Electrochemical sensors based on MIP are attractive approach to mimic natural molecular recognition by preparing synthetic recognition sites with predetermined selectivity for various target compounds. Their inherent advantages can order as molecular specificity, reusability, physiochemical stability [22–24]. The selectivity and affinities acquired from the MIP process approach those of biological recognition the selected working compounds. The high stability coupled to long-term stability and resistance to harsh environments. MIP have been combined with a wide range of electroanalytical transducers [22–24]. In MIP technique, the target compound binds as a template molecule that binds functional monomers by covalent or non-covalent bonding during the polymerization process. It is fast, cheap, highly sensitive and a viable option for miniaturization technique. The obtained macroporous polymers contain recognition sites, which have a high affinity for the printing molecule due to the shape and arrangement of the functional groups. As a result, MIP-based sensors can use easily for the rapid detection and determination of selected compounds from their dosage forms and biological materials.

To our knowledge, very limited electrochemical study is available for the determination of PMX in pharmaceutical formulations and biological samples [25–27]. In these published papers, the selectivity and the sensitivity are not enough for the PMX detection or determination from biological materials because of the possible interferences and other potential anticancer drugs, which are used, in the combined therapy to the patient. Despite of the importance and the availability of many applicable methods of PMX, there is no previous report on the electroanalytical MIP sensor.

The aim of this work is to develop a new, environmental friendly, low cost, selective and sensitive simple electrochemical sensor based on surface molecularly imprinted technology and its application for sensitive and selective assay of PMX in bulk, dosage forms and biological samples after optimization of different experimental parameters. In this study, we suggest on the producing, characterization and application of a novel electrochemical selective PMX sensor in which CQD covered SPCE was decorated with o-PD on monomer for occurring a MIP layer. The precision, accuracy, selectivity, and sensitivity of the proposed MIP sensor for PMX are also checked. The structures of thus produced electrochemical sensors were characterized and the experimental parameters influencing the performance of the sensors were investigated and optimized in detail. Because creating MIP electrochemical sensor is inexpensive, readily available, and facile to prepare, we considered it for the determination of PMX. Computational studies carried out to illuminate the redox reaction mechanisms.

Voltammetric measurements (Cyclic voltammetry (CV), differential pulse voltammetry (DPV)) were performed using a three-electrode system, consisting of the modified electrode (SPCE/CQDs/PMX imprinted poly(o-PD)) as working electrode, a platinum wire as counter electrode, and a Ag/AgCl (3.0 molL−1 KCl) as reference electrode. Voltammetric measurements were performed by using a PalmSens potentiostat (Netherlands). EIS values were recorded between 0.1 Hz and 0.1 kHz at 0.01 mV amplitude in a redox marker solution using AUTOLAB-PGSTAT100N (Eco Chemie, Utrecht, The Netherlands) electrochemical instrument. For the characterization of MIP, the modified electrodes were characterized by SEM analysis using ZEISS EVO 40 (Merlin, Carl Zeiss). For the characterization of CQDs, the modified electrodes were characterized by TEM analysis using Tecnai G2 S-TWIN (FEI, Oregon, USA) (200 kV, dropped on 200 mesh, carbon coated grid).

All chemicals used in the study were of analytical reagent grade purity and distilled water were used in the experiments. The stock solution of PMX was prepared using distilled water. PMX and o-PD were kindly supplied by Koçak Farma (Turkey) and Sigma Aldrich (USA), respectively. 0.1 molL−1 acetate buffer solution of pH 5.2 selected as a supporting electrolyte was prepared by mixing 0.05 molL−1 CH3COOH and 0.16 molL−1 CH3COONa.3H2O and pH was adjusted by HCl and NaOH [28–30]. CQD was purchased from DropSens (Llanera, Spain). A SPCE from Dropsens was consisting of a graphite-working electrode, a carbon counter electrode, and a silver pseudo-reference electrode.

Before the modification, the screen printed carbon electrode surface should be pretreated by the means of applying the potential between −0.2 and 1.6 V in 0.50 molL−1 H2SO4 for 20 cycles. Subsequently, different volume of the CQD solution were tried to find the optimum conditions. Therefore, 10.0 μL of the commercial CQD was directly dropped onto the surface of the SPCE and kept for 15 min at vacuum drying.

The electropolymerization was performed by scanning the potential of the SPCE in an 0.1 molL−1 acetate buffer (pH 5.2) containing 5 mmolL−1 o-PD and 0.5 mmolL−1 PMX by CVs (20 cycles) in the potential range of −0.20–0.75 V at a scan rate of 50 mV s−1. Afterwards, the MIP/CQD/SPCE was rinsed with distilled water and then immersed into 0.1 molL−1 NaOH for 1 min to remove the template molecules (PMX). Then the imprinted MIP/CQD/SPCE was rinsed with distilled water again. As a control, the non-imprinted polymer (NIP) modified CQD/SPCE, was fabricated following the same procedure, but in the absence of template molecules, and noted as NIP/CQD/SPCE. Before each sample measurement, the MIP/CQD/SPCE is incubated in a different concentration of PMX for 5 min. After which time, the electrode is rinsed with distilled water. Finally, sensing measurement is conducted in a solution containing 5.0 mmolL−1 Fe(CN)6 3-/4- at a ratio of 1:1 and 0.1 molL−1 KCl at 0.16 V vs. SPCE by DPV and CV techniques. The preparation schematic of the MIP/CQD/SPCE sensor is depicted in Scheme 1 . The preparation process can be ordered into the following steps:
(1)
Modification of the electrode with CQDs.
(2)
Electropolymerization of the functional monomer o-PD and template PMX to fabricate the MIP on the surface of the CQDs/GCE.
(3)
Removal of the template PMX from the molecular imprinted membranes
(4)
Rebinding of the different concentration of PMX in the cavity.

The MIP-based sensor was prepared using 0.1 mmolL−1 PMX as template and the same detection procedure was used the rebinding step was performed in 0.1 mmolL−1 solutions containing guanine, sulpride and ascorbic acid as selected similar compounds.

The developed MIP/CQD/SPCE sensor was also applied to detect PMX in spiked synthetic serum sample. For this purpose, standard solutions of human serum derived from human male AB plasma were prepared. By mixing 5.4 mL of acetonitrile, 3.6 mL of the synthetic serum sample and 1.0 mL of 10 nM PMX-solution, the stock solution containing 1 nM PMX was prepared. The purpose of acetonitrile addition was to precipitate the protein residues in the sample. Blank serum is prepared with 5.4 mL of acetonitrile, 3.6 mL of synthetic serum sample and 1.0 mL of 100 mmolL−1 acetate buffer(pH 5.2). The solutions were centrifuged for 25 min at 3000 rpm and the supernatants were then collected before the experiments. For the electrochemical analysis, aliquot from the stock solution of serum were used for rebinding of PMX. The known amount standard PMX added to synthetic serum sample. The consecutive five measurements performed with same solution and sensor. The obtained response evaluated according to relative standard deviation.

All density functional theory (DFT) calculations were exerted with the Gaussian 09 program set [35] to support the proposed mechanism for the electrode processes. The geometry of PMX was fully optimized with Becke3 (B3) Lee–Yang–Parr (LYP) method [31,32,34] (B3LYP) using 6-311G∗ basis set which adds d functions on heavy atoms [33] in gas phase. In the meantime, PMX’s frequency calculations were done at the same level to confirm the optimized geometry of pemetrexed. The MESP is employed to obtain important insights of the compound in atomic level [31–35].

The surface area of the SPCE could increase with casting CQD on SPCE. The next step was followed by o-PD polymerization process in 100 mmolL−1 acetate buffer (pH 5.2) in the presence of monomer as 5 mmolL−1 o-PD and template as 0.5 mmolL−1 PMX by multiple CV cycles (20) in the potential range of −0.20–0.75 V vs. Ag/AgCl at a scan rate of 50 mV s−1 (Fig. 1 ). To obtain NIP formation, the same protocol has been applied without template. In Fig. 1, a well-defined oxidation response was observed at around 0.2 V vs. Ag/AgCl on CQD/SPCE, corresponding to the anodic oxidation of the o-PD. This oxidation response sharply decreased after consecutive scans of polymerization of o-PD because of nonconductive film formation on CQD/SPCE.

The possible interaction mechanism between PMX and poly o-PD might be occur by H bonds and electrostatic interactions to selective binding of PMX to MIP (Fig. 2 ). The electrochemical active group of PMX could be suggested by indole ring according to previous studies with indole compound in aqueous solutions [26,36–40].
MIP/CQD/SPCE have many advantages in electrochemical determination field such as possesses good selectivity and sensitivity, extraordinary good reproducibility.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of conditions for developing sensitive and selective MIP based sensor
ce:section.ce:para: The number of electropolymerization scan cycles during the preparation of electrode surface affected the sensitivity of the electrochemical sensor. The thickness of the imprinted o-PD layer can related with the amount of template molecules embedded which then influences the sensitivity of the sensor. o-PD is attractive due to the good homogeneity and high physical, thermal and mechanic stability of the formed polymer [41]. A series of experiments in which electrodes fabricated with different numbers of CV cycles were carried out to obtain the optimal number of CV cycles in the electropolymerization process. Thus, in this work, it was decided that 20 cycles were the most appropriate number of cycles to produce a membrane with optimum analytical performances (Fig. 3 ).

The binding sites and recognition capability of MIP film is considerably effected by the molar ratio of functional monomer/template (Fig. 4 a). The current signal of imprinted electrode were recorded with molar ratio of monomer to template as 2.5:1, 5: 1, 10:1, 20:1. As a result, the molar ratio of functional monomer/template is selected as 10:1 for this study.

It is considerably important to elute the template completely to become satisfactory reproducibility, sensitivity and selectivity. The electrode was immersed in 0.1 molL−1 NaOH and the immersion time to removal the analyte was optimized (Fig. 4b). These results showed that the immersion of the sensor in 0.1 molL−1 NaOH for 60 s was enough for complete removal of the PMX from the MIP structure.

The incubation time of PMX is a crucial step for developing the PMX sensor. After the template was yanked out the layer, the MIP/CQD/SPCE was incubated with PMX from 2.5 to 10 min (Fig. 5 ). It shows that the MIP based sensor have high affinity of the imprinted film and recognizing ability the PMX molecule. Therefore, optimized incubation time was found as 5 for the determination of PMX.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of developed MIP based sensor
ce:section.ce:para: CV and EIS techniques were performed to characterize the stepwise imprinted sensor. The electrochemical response of the MIP based sensor significantly related with the effective casting of its MIP template. CV is the guiding method to follow the electrons transfer reaction of the electrode surface and to obtain information about surrounding film on it. Thus, the electrochemical performance of the MIP/CQDs/SPCE was investigated by CV in the presence of redox marker. The electrode surface can be expanded by the means of modifying electrode surface with CQDs nanocomposite.

With the consecutive rising of scan cycles, a polymer layer was formed on electrode surface, and the redox marker signal was decreased considerably because of the restricted electron transfer between the electrolyte and surface of the electrode [42]. After removal of PMX molecules, the signal of redox marker was appeared. When the template is washed out, the cavity become suitable for rebinding the same molecules. For this reason, the removal step of MIP technique is a quietly important step in the preparation of MIPs. In the rebinding step of MIP technique, the signal of redox marker, which is suppression of the Fe(CN)6 3-/4- oxidation-reduction process are related with respect to PMX concentration (Fig. 6 A). EIS technique illuminate the electron transfer properties between electrode surface and solution. Each step, charge transfer resistance (Rct) values across the interface of bare/SPCE, CQDs/SPCE; MIP/CQD/SPCE after EP; MIP/CQD/SPCE after PMX removal; MIP/CQD/SPCE after PMX rebinding were measured. The EIS response formed in a semicircular part and a linear part. MIP/CQD/SPCE showed least Rct because of the conductivity of the CQD modified electrode (Fig. 6B).

According to MIP studies [24,43,44], TEM and SEM techniques have been commonly utilized for characterization of electrode surface. The polymer beads were characterized to validate this preparation concept. The corresponding sizes and morphologies of the CQDs/SPCE and MIP/CQDs/SPCE were evaluated by the recorded SEM images in Fig. 7 . The SEM images also confirmed, as seen in Fig. 7A, that the surface of the bare SPCE exhibited a relatively flat structure with little roughness. Using SEM imaging, Fig. 7B and C has proven to be the CQDs and MIP layer distributed almost homogeneously on the surface of the SPCE, and forming with roughness and wrinkles. In Fig. 7D shows the TEM image of CQDs. CQDs have the crystalline and amorphous of carbon. Using TEM, a particle size was obtained as 10 nm, approximately. A large number of effective and tailor-made imprinted sites were found on the surface of the MIPs. Furthermore, the surface of the MIPs was rough and irregular due to their imprinted cavity.

Furthermore, rebinding studies were performed using different concentrations(1, 2, 5, 10, 20 nmolL−1 PMX) of PMX in synthetic serum sample. In human serum sample studies, differential pulse voltammograms of PMX were recorded in 5 mmolL−1 [Fe(CN)6]3−/4– mixture in 0.1 M KCl. In synthetic serum sample, the dependence of the DPV responses on the concentration of PMX( Fig. 9 a) and relationship between peak current and PMX concentration (Fig. 9b) for MIP/CQD/SPCE are presented in Fig. 9.

The recovery performance of developed MIP based sensor was checked by adding standard (5.0 nmolL−1) PMX into synthetic serum sample. According to results, good recoveries were observed (Table 2 ) with 1.42% relative standard deviation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Precision and shelf-life of the MIP based sensor
ce:section.ce:para: In order to check the between-day measurements of the developed sensor, several modified SPCEs were fabricated and applied to the analysis of a 0.1 mmolL−1 PMX solution. The relative standard deviations for reproducibility of MIP based sensor were found as 1.61%. This shows that the precision of the method and sensor is high.

To check the shelf-life of MIP/CQD/SPCE during 3 days. The relative standard deviation of sensor in 3 days was obtained as 1.40%. The results showed there was no meaningless difference between responses of electrodes in the presence of PMX.

The selectivity of the MIP sensor was also evaluated in the presence of three compounds (0.1 mmolL−1): guanine, sulpride and ascorbic acid, whose structures are similar or a part of PMX (Fig. 10 ). The incubation with these compounds led to no significant change on MIP based sensor. They showed low affinity and could not bind to cavity of template PMX. Hence, we can say that our proposed sensor is selective to PMX structure.
Up to date, the several studies performed on PMX detection, but there is no any development of selective sensor (Table 3 ).

In this study, besides PMX experimental studies, theoretical calculations applied to elucidate the relevant parts of the compound in the applied electrochemical techniques. Therefore, the PMX structure is geometrically and energetically optimized at the DFT/B3LYP/6-311 G∗ level to estimate the boundary molecular orbitals and electronic structure of PMX using the Gaussian 09 program.

The MESP was also computed to investigate the electron density of PMX. Generated MESP map shows the relation between molecular structure and physicochemical property of PMX, (Fig. 11 ). Different colors show and visualize different electrostatic potentials at the surface of the structure. The region of attractive potential appears in red and those of repulsion potential appear in blue thus the carbonyl and O–H regions have higher positive electrostatic potential and participate in nucleophilic reactions. N atoms with higher electronegative values have higher electron density around them and participate in electrophilic attacks.

Anticancer drug, PMX was selectively determined on MIP based sensor. The surface characterized by surface and electrochemical techniques. All conditions for successful electropolymerization and construction of imprinting of PMX optimized. As a result, the developed sensor had successfully affinity to PMX instead of similar molecules such as guanine, sulpride and ascorbic acid. MIP based sensor for PMX showed high sensitivity, wide working linear range and high recovery values. Besides these applied studies, computational application, MESP were probed by using Gaussian 09. These computational findings support the experimental applications of PMX.
Development and validation of high performance liquid chromatographic and uv spectrophotometric method for estimation of pemetrexed disodium in bulk drug and pharmaceutical formulation.

G.O., N.K.B, T.T.T., S.A. conceived and planned the experiments. G.O carried out the experiment. S.A. verified the analytical methods. N.K.B. contributed to the interpretation of the results. T.T.T. calculate molecular electrostatic potential surface (MESP) and evaluate the effects of the electronic and structure properties of PMX. All authors provided critical feedback and helped shape the research, analysis and manuscript.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
